title: Why can't Apple design its own AI chip?
gist: A SemiWiki forum poster relays a report that Apple's M2 Ultra servers struggle to run large models like Google's Gemini (forcing Apple to run parts of Siri on Nvidia GPUs within Google Cloud) and that Apple is reportedly shopping for AI semiconductor startup acquisitions rather than designing its own inference chip; the post is an open question to the community, not a resolved analysis.

insights.claim: According to a report cited in the post, Apple's custom M2 Ultra servers struggle to run large AI models such as Google's Gemini, forcing Apple to run parts of its upgraded Siri assistant on Nvidia GPUs within Google Cloud rather than on its own silicon.
insights.speaker: P
insights.so_what: If accurate, this indicates Apple's in-house AI accelerator strategy has fallen short of hyperscaler-grade inference needs, implying continued reliance on Nvidia/Google infrastructure for its flagship AI assistant despite Apple's silicon self-sufficiency reputation.
insights.quote: Apple's custom M2 Ultra servers struggle to run large AI models like Google's Gemini, forcing the company to run parts of its upgraded Siri assistant on Nvidia GPUs within Google Cloud

insights.claim: Apple is reportedly seeking to acquire AI semiconductor startups to strengthen its cloud server AI-inference capabilities rather than building or partnering for a custom inference chip the way Amazon, Google, and OpenAI (with Broadcom) have done.
insights.speaker: P
insights.so_what: An M&A-led AI-chip strategy (versus organic design or a Broadcom-style partnership) would be a distinct competitive signal worth watching for potential Apple acquisition targets in the AI accelerator space.
insights.quote: Apple is reportedly seeking to acquire AI semiconductor startups to strengthen its cloud server capabilities.
